items.title: New film highlights ESRC research on online harms
items.summary: HateLab, led by Professor Matthew Williams of Cardiff University, has shown that spikes in online hate speech can be followed by increases in hate crime offline.

items.title: Mars's 2018 planetwide dust storm hints at possible solar influence on its weather
items.summary: Solar storms may affect weather on Mars when they strike at the same time as major Martian dust storms, a new study suggests for the first time. Researchers found evidence of rising temperatures in the red planet's lower atmosphere when the two events coincided—hinting that Mars may have a more

items.title: Upside of online relationships
items.summary: Last fall, students filed into Vilas Hall for the first session of Online Communication and Personal Relationships, a class with the potential to improve their friendships and family relationships. There was even a chance it could help them meet the love of their lives while boosting their mental he

items.title: Students' emotional resources in high school linked to college completion
items.summary: Academic achievement in high school has long been viewed as one of the strongest predictors of college success. But a new study from Penn State researchers suggests that students' emotional resources—including their motivation, persistence and perceptions of school climate—may also play an impo

items.title: AI agent helps prepare synchrotron X-ray experimental measurements, paving the way for autonomous operation
items.summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) models are now used daily by many people worldwide, both for professional and personal purposes. Over the past decades, scientists specialized in various disciplines have also started using these models to conduct research or simplify their experimental practices.

items.title: Invisible microbes 'mining' toxic waste in the Flinders Ranges
items.summary: New research from Monash University revealed that microscopic life is actively breaking down decades-old mining waste in South Australia, turning stable radioactive and toxic metals into mobile nanoparticles that can easily travel through the environment.

items.title: The best way to measure fire risk might be underground
items.summary: When fire risk is high, Arizonans know the major warning signs: hot temperatures, low humidity and lots of wind.

items.title: For consumers, distrust outweighs trust
items.summary: Anyone who has opened the door of a vacation rental after a long trip, only to find it hasn't been cleaned as promised, knows the burn of betrayal. That traveler is more likely to distrust the proprietor of their next vacation rental, according to new research from Annabelle Roberts, assistant

items.title: Aligned graphite particles unlock stable levitation above magnets, study finds
items.summary: A diamagnetic substance is slightly repelled by magnetic fields. With a strong enough magnet, the diamagnetic force can override gravity, and the substance will float in the air. Graphite, the main component of pencil lead, is considered one of the best substances for such real-world levitation, and

items.title: Astronomers catch a recurring black hole eruption in real time
items.summary: For the third time in 35 years, the black hole at the center of the galaxy IC 3599 has erupted, and this time, astronomers were watching it closely. IC 3599, a galaxy roughly 280 million light-years away, has flared this dramatically twice before, in 1990 and 2010, but both eruptions were only piece

items.title: Indigenous knowledge key to building healthier and more sustainable food systems
items.summary: Traditional and Indigenous knowledge could play a vital role in addressing some of the world's most pressing challenges, including climate change, biodiversity loss and poor health and nutrition, according to a new review of current research.

items.title: Great Barrier Reef at risk of 'collapse', scientists warn
items.summary: Australia's Great Barrier Reef is at very high risk of extreme and widespread bleaching and "ecosystem collapse" linked to this year's El Niño weather phenomenon, leading scientists warned Tuesday.

items.title: Znanstvenici razvili metodu uzgoja umjetnih krvnih žila
items.summary: ISTRAŽIVAČI s MIT-a razvili su metodu za precizan uzgoj umjetnih krvnih žila pomoću magnetskih sila. Ova tehnika omogućuje kontrolu smjera i duljine rasta kapilara, što je ključno za tkivni inženjering.

items.title: Fires in Brazil's Amazon dropped to historic low in 2025
items.summary: Forest fires in the Brazilian Amazon fell to a historic low in 2025 after peaking the previous year, according to a report published Tuesday.

items.title: Why Maine’s Sandy Shorelines Turn Jagged
items.summary: Differences in the underlying bedrock and how rivers distribute sediment make the coastlines west of Portland look unlike those northeast of the city.

items.title: Memorable visuals drive online ad purchases more than emotion, study suggests
items.summary: A study of digital advertising in the International Journal of Business and Systems Research suggests that memorable visuals are more likely to encourage consumers to buy than campaigns that rely primarily on emotional impact. The findings challenge conventional wisdom about how online advertising i

items.title: Envisioning the next 150 years of crop research with the Alma Mater Plots
items.summary: A couple of miles south of the main quad at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, a remarkable agricultural experiment is taking shape. The so-called Alma Mater Plots are unique in their setup—with 64 plots of 1 acre (0.4 hectares) each, individually tile-drained, it is the largest experiment

items.title: Q&A: How anaerobic digestion can help reduce emissions and increase income streams
items.summary: An advanced technology that could benefit farmers' bottom line and help meet the rising demand for domestic energy production borrows a key ingredient from a centuries-old practice of burning dried manure to warm homes where trees were sparse or expensive: animal waste.

items.title: Eight lifesaving ways to improve wildfire risk management
items.summary: As the flames advanced toward the village of Bédar in Spain's Almería province, the mayor gave a desperate order to ring the church bells to warn residents. It was not enough. The July 9 Los Gallardos fire, which claimed 13 lives and burned 7,000 hectares (17,300 acres), was Spain's third-

items.title: Decade of research reshapes multilingual learner policy
items.summary: A lot of high school students load up on Advanced Placement courses to strengthen their college applications. But for many California students classified as English learners, access to even the basic college-preparatory courses required for admission to the state's public universities can remai

items.title: Students enable widespread water monitoring in India
items.summary: When a local community gets involved in environmental monitoring, its efforts can build awareness of local environmental issues and provide more abundant data than scientists can collect on their own. But how does the quality of the data compare with readings taken by professionals?

items.title: Unusual three-way bestows magnetic sense to single-celled organism
items.summary: How do microorganisms orient themselves to find the optimal habitat? Scientists have thoroughly investigated so-called magnetotactic bacteria, which use the Earth's magnetic field as a biological compass. Some eukaryotic single-celled organisms—that is, more complex organisms like ciliates, whi

items.title: One idea, two cosmic mysteries—linking Little Red Dots and globular clusters
items.summary: A new study led by astronomers at The University of Texas at Austin proposes a theory that could solve two astronomical riddles at once: the nature of Little Red Dots and the origin of globular clusters. Rather than representing distinct objects, the study suggests that one may instead be the ancest

items.title: Transforming vibrations into clean fuels and chemicals through piezosynthesis
items.summary: From ocean waves and flowing rivers to the systems used to transport and treat water, vibrations are everywhere. But can vibrations do more than just shake water? Can they split water or drive the production of useful chemicals from water? A research team led by Professor Sai Kishore Ravi from the S

items.title: New cell imaging method measures protein folding in living organelles
items.summary: Protein aggregates are associated with the development of diseases like Alzheimer's and Parkinson's. These are found more often in certain areas of cells known as membraneless organelles. Do these organelles promote this protein clumping, or do they protect the cell by temporarily absorbin

items.title: 'Narcissist' bosses are more likely to oppose work from home, studies show. But are they right?
items.summary: Have you ever wondered why your boss wants you back in the office full-time, rather than allowing hybrid work, where you can combine work from home and in an office? A fascinating study just shed fresh light on this topic.

items.title: 30,000 people tried for bigamy over the past 400 years in England and Wales, archives reveal
items.summary: Bigamy has been a rare crime during the past 400 years—and offenders have been harshly punished. It seems likely that no more than 30,000 individuals have ever been tried for bigamy in England and Wales during this period. Most have been convicted, and possible punishment has ranged from execution,

items.title: Molecules stop carbon nanotubes clumping, unlocking record heat-to-electricity performance
items.summary: QUT researchers have overcome a challenge that has limited next-generation energy-harvesting materials for more than two decades, opening the door to more powerful wearable electronics and new ways of turning wasted heat into electricity. The breakthrough centers on carbon nanotubes, which are flexi

items.title: Carbon nanotube model reveals how smart sensors perform at aircraft-like extremes
items.summary: Researchers from Skoltech, in collaboration with the Harbin Institute of Technology and Jiangsu University (China), have published a study presenting the first empirical model to explain and predict the sensing behavior of hierarchical tri-phase carbon nanotube systems across a wide temperature rang

items.title: Early career funding improves scientific influence and citations for women
items.summary: Early-career funding has a bigger impact on women than men when it comes to their careers in science, according to a new study from City St George's, University of London. While the study found that successful applicants in both groups secure more future research funding than unsuccessful appli

items.title: Ancestral variation, not new mutations, may impact survival of species facing climate change
items.summary: Research co-authored by an Oklahoma Museum of Natural History curator, her graduate student and an international team of scientists has shed new light on the evolutionary "toolkit" animals use to survive environmental change.

items.title: Heat shock reveals rapid protein-control system cells may use to survive
items.summary: The planet is getting hotter, and life will need to adapt to rising temperatures to survive. One way cells adapt to changing conditions is by synthesizing new proteins. That works well when the environment changes slowly, but if a crisis hits, like a sudden temperature increase, cells cannot wait fo

items.title: How rainfall timing can dramatically reshape entire plant communities
items.summary: Every spring, millions of people around the world head outdoors to admire wildflowers. But flowering is much more than a beautiful seasonal display. It is one of the most important stages in a plant's life cycle, determining not only its ability to reproduce but also the survival of pollinators

items.title: Light-driven chemistry steers electron transfers beyond redox limits
items.summary: Chemists use single-electron transfers to synthesize complex, ring-shaped molecular structures found in many drug candidates and advanced materials, but current techniques still have limitations. A new study, accepted for publication in Nature, describes a technique that could steer these chemical r

items.title: Acid-resistant nanocage shows promise for targeted gastric cancer therapy
items.summary: Researchers from the Yong Loo Lin School of Medicine, National University of Singapore (NUS Medicine), have developed a swallowable nanoscale delivery platform designed to transport therapeutic enzymes through the stomach's acidic environment and activate a cancer-killing reaction at tumor site

items.title: Oystercatchers, known for sharp bills and beady eyes, are making a comeback thanks to conservation
items.summary: On a pristine Rhode Island beach teeming with noisy seabirds, a group of biologists armed with nets sneak up on a pair of American oystercatcher chicks.

items.title: Znanstvenici: Majmuni imaju geometrijske vještine slične djeci
items.summary: NOVO istraživanje pokazuje da majmuni imaju geometrijske vještine slične ljudskim predškolcima, što upućuje na zajedničke kognitivne temelje. Formalno obrazovanje nadograđuje te urođene sposobnosti.

items.title: NASA Sets Briefings for SpaceX Crew-13 Mission to Space Station
items.summary: NASA and its partners will discuss the upcoming crew rotation mission to the International Space Station during a pair of news conferences on Monday, Aug. 3, from the agency’s Johnson Space Center in Houston. Mission leadership will provide an overview of NASA’s SpaceX Crew‑13 mission at 12 p.m. EDT

items.title: New web tool maps fossil locations across ancient continents in seconds
items.summary: Calculating a fossil's position throughout geological history is a complex process. In paleontology and other disciplines, determining a location in the past is a challenge that requires refined computational tools to process large volumes of data. Now, the BIOST3 Research Group at the Universi

items.title: Virtual reality reveals the role of stress in selecting the memories we store and the details we remember
items.summary: The brain is the machine that perceives, analyzes, sorts, stores and prioritizes our memories. However, not all experiences and perceptions are stored in the same way, and not everything is remembered equally, especially when under pressure or emotional stress. A new research study carried out by re

items.title: Canada faces one of its most intense fire seasons
items.summary: With nearly 1,000 active fires and an area as large as Belgium already burned, Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney says his country is facing one of the "most intense" wildfire seasons in its history.

items.title: Surfing webcams help demystify Oregon's coastal fog
items.summary: A summer beach day in Oregon often involves a cool, gray blanket of fog that not only shapes the experience of beachgoers but also affects the coastal and inland environment. Now, commercially available surfing webcams are helping scientists measure Oregon's coastal fog, according to a study fr

items.title: Move over quokkas—Rottnest's salt lake bacteria could be the island's next big claim to fame
items.summary: Scientists at Murdoch University have read the full genetic code of bacteria from the salt lakes of Wadjemup (Rottnest Island), giving them their clearest picture yet of how these tiny organisms could be used to make biodegradable plastic. Their research is published in the journal Microbial Genomic

items.title: Now that the World Cup is over, can host cities turn their investment into a lasting social impact?
items.summary: The 2026 World Cup has been a real moneymaker for FIFA. Revenue from TV broadcasting rights, ticket sales and other commercial sources is projected to amount to nearly $11 billion, which would make it the most financially successful event in the international soccer governing body's history.

items.title: Children and monkeys share common understanding of geometry
items.summary: With their ability to use tools, remember faces and even learn how to communicate with humans through sign language, nonhuman primates are considered to be among the smartest creatures on Earth. However, some concepts—geometry, for instance—have traditionally been thought of as beyond the abilities

items.title: What explains gender pay gaps in academia?
items.summary: Gender pay gaps in academia are well documented. The harder question is why they persist. A new study published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences tested several leading answers to that question.

items.title: Why do some people who trust in science also believe in scientific conspiracy theories?
items.summary: In a world where trust in institutions is generally declining, trust in science remains relatively high. Yet belief in scientific conspiracy theories, such as hushed-up effects of vaccines or airplane "chemtrails," is also widespread.

items.title: Arctic winter sea ice returns to significant decline, ending the temporary slowdown
items.summary: New analysis shows that a "pause" in Arctic winter sea ice decline seen in the early 2020s has ended following record lows in 2025 and early 2026. The study, led by the University of Southampton in collaboration with the National Oceanography Center (NOC), adds the most recent satellite observations

items.title: NASA, GE Aerospace Work Enables Hybrid-Electric Flight Demonstration
items.summary: An aircraft powered by a megawatt-class hybrid-electric engine developed in collaboration with NASA and built by GE Aerospace, demonstrated flight of an innovation that can inform new generations of fuel-saving aircraft power systems. Mounted to a Saab 340B aircraft, the engine flew at Farnborough I

items.summary: An aircraft powered by a megawatt-class hybrid-electric engine developed in collaboration with NASA and built by GE Aerospace, demonstrated flight of an innovation that can inform new generations of fuel-saving aircraft power systems. items.title: Researchers complete new assessments of vulnerable Southern Ocean species
items.summary: Postdoctoral researcher Renato Borras-Chavez's area of study—pinnipeds in the Southern Ocean—often takes him far from the University of Rhode Island campus. Now, the researcher in Sarah Kienle's CEAL Lab is sharing more about declining populations of certain seals and will present on the t

items.title: Japan's first mosasaur premaxilla fossil hints at a new species
items.summary: A research team from Okayama University of Science, Tokyo City University, the Natural History Museum, Kishiwada City (Osaka Prefecture), and other institutions has identified four previously unrecognized fossil bones from a rock containing mosasaur remains collected about 30 years ago in Kishiwada

items.title: New NASA Earth Missions Gear Up to Start Science Flights
items.summary: The first of six new airborne campaigns will hit the skies this summer.

items.title: There's no right or wrong way to say 'Appalachia'
items.summary: People pronounce the word "Appalachia" differently, and much of that contrast depends on where they live. But no matter how you say it, a Virginia Tech project shows that no one way trumps all. The differences are rooted in historical and community influences.

items.title: Why some human brains can outlast other soft tissues after death
items.summary: Why do some brains survive long after death when most other soft tissue decays? That's the question a research team led by Alexandra Morton-Hayward at the University of Oxford set out to answer.

items.title: Greater nutrient variety can counterintuitively shrink microbial diversity, experiments reveal
items.summary: Microbial communities are considered particularly diverse—in soil, water and the human body. A common assumption in ecology is that the greater the diversity of available nutrients, the more species can coexist. A research team at the University of Tübingen has now shown that the opposite can be tru

items.title: Investing podcasts are super popular. But do they help people learn the market?
items.summary: From game highlights to cold cases to political pontificating, podcasts provide the stage for public conversations in 2026. But are we learning anything from all that chatter? When it comes to investing and playing the markets, the answer seems to be yes. New research from the University of Georgia

items.title: World's first gene edited Culicoides biting midges open door for livestock disease research
items.summary: Scientists have successfully edited the genome of biting midges, opening the door to new research on how these insects transmit disease. Culicoides biting midges are small blood-feeding insects responsible for spreading important vector-borne viruses such as bluetongue virus (BTV), Schmallenberg vir

items.title: Research reveals how human bias shapes social media feeds
items.summary: People are naturally attracted to excitement. According to new Virginia Tech research, the same is true on social media, where feeds are overrun with international travel, skydiving and other experiences that may seem out of the ordinary.

items.title: Study could help event organizers prevent crowd crushes and stampedes
items.summary: McGill researchers have found that dangerous crowd conditions leading to surges and stampedes may not emerge suddenly but instead develop gradually, as repeated physical contact and pushing accumulate and become synchronized among nearby individuals. Recognizing how these conditions arise could help

items.title: Rare sighting reveals Arctic bobtail squid and egg masses in subzero Greenland fjord
items.summary: Marine biologists have observed the Arctic bobtail squid in its natural habitat in the North Atlantic Ocean for the first time, including the rare discovery of a mature female with eggs. Dr Paige Maroni, from The University of Western Australia's School of Biological Sciences, is lead author of

items.title: Microplastic tire pollution found in Brisbane balcony dust
items.summary: Tire particles and potentially toxic tire chemical additives have been found in dust collected from Brisbane residential balconies. University of Queensland researchers examined the balcony dust from homes across inner-city, suburban and near-road settings to see how traffic-related pollution travel

items.title: Shroud of Turin DNA sheds light on its biological history
items.summary: New research has uncovered the complex biological history preserved within DNA traces found on the Shroud of Turin, offering fresh insights into one of the world's most studied and debated historical artifacts.

items.title: Understanding how wild plants handle shifting disease threats, conditions
items.summary: St. Louis is a plant science powerhouse, but outside the neatly tended rows of crops and gleaming greenhouses sits another treasure trove of biological data: The wild plants tucked into yards, sidewalk cracks, ditches and fields have much to reveal. items.title: AI falls short on context and cultural rhetoric in United Nations speech translations
items.summary: As generative artificial intelligence (AI) is increasingly used in translation, questions have been raised about whether it could eventually replace professional interpreters. items.title: Exploring the use of accounts to manage reluctant participation in microteaching sessions
items.summary: Classrooms often fall silent when the teacher poses a question. The pause is not always due to a lack of knowledge. items.title: Researchers challenge evidence behind high-profile parental alienation study
items.summary: Researchers from Manchester and Canada have shown that the claim by the authors of a study published earlier this year that most of its participants had experienced parental alienating behaviors during childhood is seriously misleading. Claims in the study that people affected by parental alienating

items.title: From Hampton to Mars: How NASA Langley Helped Land on the Red Planet
items.summary: Fifty years ago, NASA’s Viking 1 and 2 landers made the first successful landings on Mars, opening a new era in planetary exploration. Behind that achievement was a team at NASA’s Langley Research Center in Hampton, Virginia, whose steady leadership and technical expertise helped turn an ambitious i

items.title: Red tape and conflicting priorities limit the impact of social businesses, research finds
items.summary: Social businesses are being prevented from delivering lasting benefits to their regions because of complex policies, unclear guidance, financial constraints and poor use of resources, according to new research from the Durham University Business School. items.title: Bee brains reveal how colonies divide work without central planning

In an article published in the Proceedings of the National Academy

items.title: Our First View of the Surface of Mars
items.summary: “Touchdown, we have touchdown!” At 5:12 a.m. PDT, July 20, 1976, mission controllers at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory erupted in cheers as they learned that the Viking 1 lander had survived its descent through the thin Martian atmosphere. Forty minutes later, the lander’s first image began to app

items.title: Food labeling combined with higher prices for carbon-intensive foods could help reduce UK greenhouse gases by 5%
items.summary: Economists say labeling supermarket food to show its environmental impact, combined with taxing the most carbon-intensive items, could slash UK greenhouse gas emissions by almost 5% while generating net gains for society.

items.title: AI may not make us stupid, but relying on it could weaken the skills built through practice
items.summary: The worry of becoming stupid isn't new; it dates back to the time of the Greek philosopher Socrates, who insisted that writing would destroy memory and weaken the mind. items.title: Astronauti su s Mjeseca donijeli kamenje. Još uvijek otkrivamo njihove tajne
items.summary: PRIJE 57 godina, misije Apollo donijele su mjesečevo kamenje koje je otkrilo tajne o postanku Mjeseca i Zemlje. Ti uzorci i danas pokreću znanstvena otkrića i inspiriraju nove misije poput Artemisa.

items.title: Američka obitelj tuži OpenAI: "ChatGPT je potaknuo 29-godišnju majku na samoubojstvo"
items.summary: OBITELJ 29-godišnjakinje koja je počinila samoubojstvo tuži OpenAI. Tvrde da je chatbot GPT-4o poticao njezine religijske zablude i ohrabrivao je da si oduzme život nakon intenzivne komunikacije.

items.title: Beyond proximity: New policy model to transform access to parks
items.summary: Accessible urban green spaces could save the NHS up to £2.1 billion each year, but current policy fails to ensure parks are truly accessible, research shows. A new policy brief published by the university's Institute for Policy Research (IPR) sets out how a different approach could boost public

items.title: Prescribed burns may be lethal for lizards
items.summary: Scientists are calling for wildlife surveys to be conducted within hours of prescribed burns after new research revealed that evidence of animal deaths can disappear in less than two weeks, significantly underestimating the true toll of fire on Australia's wildlife.

items.title: New machine-learning tool improves accuracy of genomics research
items.summary: University of Virginia School of Medicine scientists have identified a widespread source of error in a popular method for studying the genome and created a machine-learning tool to correct it. The free tool could improve the reliability of both conventional and single-cell data generated using this

items.title: Deep-space CubeSat could stretch solar storm warnings from 15 minutes to 3 hours
items.summary: Space weather warnings could be received hours before Earth is hit rather than minutes with the help of a miniature UK-built instrument soon to be stationed in deep space. MAGIC (MAGnetometer from Imperial College) is part of the European Space Agency's Heliospheric Pioneer for Solar and Interp

items.title: Can you guess your favorite World Cup team based on 'signature' passes?
items.summary: Each World Cup team's passing patterns reveal distinctive "signatures" that show whether a team relies heavily on its star player or focuses its strategy on playing against an opponent's strengths, data shows.

items.title: Archaeological dig reveals full extent of Roman villa with intricate mosaic and bath house
items.summary: Archaeologists have uncovered a rare and intricate polychrome mosaic within the remains of a Roman villa beneath Devon farmland. items.title: Giving bees a probiotic diet could help protect them from extreme temperatures
items.summary: Honeybees do not like cold weather. When temperatures dip, they can become sluggish and unable to fly. But no matter the conditions in the outside world, they try to keep their hives close to a comfortable 34°C–36°C (93°F–97°F). However, extreme weather, both hot and cold, can overwhelm this interna

items.title: Q&A: How do federal taxes impact local schools and services?
items.summary: Increases to the standard federal tax deduction implemented in 2018 were made permanent a year ago, and researchers are beginning to understand the downstream effects on state and local governments. The increased deduction meant many taxpayers received a bigger tax break by taking the standard deduc

items.title: Swiss researchers hoard vast trove of NASA climate data
items.summary: Researchers have copied massive quantities of publicly available climate and environmental data from NASA onto a Swiss supercomputer to train artificial intelligence models—and for safekeeping amid U.S. funding cuts.

items.title: Wealthier North Carolina neighborhoods may be hotspots for insecticide-resistant tiger mosquitoes
items.summary: The choices people make to control mosquitoes in their own backyards may be helping some of those mosquitoes survive the very insecticides meant to kill them, according to two new studies from North Carolina State University. The paper on the emergence and spatial distribution of resistance appears

items.title: Why you let down your guard on ads when scrolling on social media
items.summary: My wife stared at me in shock as I ripped open the package. The FedEx driver hadn't even made it back to his truck as I held the item up overhead like a trophy. Fumbling for words, she asked: "Why on earth did you buy a Speedo … with dinosaur tear-away trunks?"

items.title: Some dinosaurs could rise like giants until they grew too big
items.summary: Two elephant-sized South American sauropods were unusually well built for standing on their hind legs. Digital tests showed that their robust femurs handled the enormous forces better than those of larger species, especially while the dinosaurs were young. The upright pose may have helped them reach

items.title: Where do the world's biggest diamonds come from? Geology now has answers
items.summary: Some of the most extraordinary gems ever found belong to a rare class of diamonds known as CLIPPIRs (Cullinan-like, Large, Inclusion-Poor, Pure, Irregular, Resorbed). They make up less than 1% of all diamonds on Earth and include three of the largest diamonds ever recovered: the Cullinan, at 3,106 c

items.title: How the most widespread parasite on Earth reads its genome
items.summary: A parasite carried by billions of people worldwide often causes harmful infections during pregnancy and in immunocompromised individuals and is a leading infectious cause of blindness in South America. Once it enters the body, it can rapidly multiply, spreading from one cell to the next.
